zh.lifeSummary: 當Isabel de Harcourt於1385年出生在英國萊希斯特斯爾馬可特 博斯沃斯時，她的父親Sir Thomas Harcourt43歲，母親Maud Grey40歲。她於大約1400年與Walter Cokesey結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和1個女兒的父母。她去世於1416年享年31歲。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Harcourt
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin) and French: habitational name from any of the places in Eure and Calvados named Harcourt, from Old French cour(t) ‘court, farmyard’ (see Court ) with an obscure first element. This surname is very rare in France.

English: habitational name from either of two places in Shropshire named Harcourt. The one near Cleobury Mortimer gets the name from Old English heafocere ‘hawker, falconer’ + cot ‘hut, cottage’; the one near Wem has as its first element Old English hearpere (see Harper ).

History: The surname of French origin (see 1 above) is listed along with the equally rare or even extinct variants or altered forms Haraucourt, Herancourt, and Héraucourt in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors.

variants.name: Court
meanings: English: topographic or occupational name from Middle English curt, court ‘large house’ (a word of the same ultimately Latin origin as 2 below), denoting someone who lived or worked in a manor house or castle, a courtly retainer.
French: topographic name from Old French co(u)rt ‘court, farmyard’ (from Latin cohors, cors, genitive cohortis, cortis ‘court, farmyard, enclosure’).

English and French: nickname for a physically small man, or ironically for a large man, from the adjective Middle English curt, Old French co(u)rt ‘short, small’ (from Latin curtus ‘curtailed, truncated, cut short, broken off’). Compare French Lecourt .

nameMeanings.name: Isabel
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Originally a Spanish version of   Elizabeth  , which was coined by deletion of the first syllable and alteration of the final consonant sound to one that can normally end a word in Spanish. The name was imported into France in the early Middle Ages, and thence into England. It was a royal name, and its enduring popularity may have been enhanced by the fact that it was borne by a queen of England—  Isabella  (  1296–1358 ), daughter of  Philip IV  of France—even though she led a turbulent life and eventually had her husband,  Edward II , murdered.

variants.name: Isabell
meanings: Originally a Spanish version of   Elizabeth  , which was coined by deletion of the first syllable and alteration of the final consonant sound to one that can normally end a word in Spanish. The name was imported into France in the early Middle Ages, and thence into England. It was a royal name, and its enduring popularity may have been enhanced by the fact that it was borne by a queen of England—  Isabella  (  1296–1358 ), daughter of  Philip IV  of France—even though she led a turbulent life and eventually had her husband,  Edward II , murdered.

variants.name: Isabelle
meanings: form of   Isabel  , now in frequent use in the English-speaking world.

variants.name: Isobella
meanings: form of   Isabel  , which has been popular in England since the 18th century and has recently become the preferred form of the name.

variants.name: Isobelle
meanings: Originally a Spanish version of   Elizabeth  , which was coined by deletion of the first syllable and alteration of the final consonant sound to one that can normally end a word in Spanish. The name was imported into France in the early Middle Ages, and thence into England. It was a royal name, and its enduring popularity may have been enhanced by the fact that it was borne by a queen of England—  Isabella  (  1296–1358 ), daughter of  Philip IV  of France—even though she led a turbulent life and eventually had her husband,  Edward II , murdered.

en.lifeSummary: When Isabel de Harcourt was born in 1385, in Market Bosworth, Leicestershire, England, her father, Sir Thomas Harcourt, was 43 and her mother, Maud Grey, was 40. She married Walter Cokesey about 1400. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 1 daughter. She died in 1416, at the age of 31.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Isabel de Harcourt est née en 1385, à Market Bosworth, Leicestershire, Angleterre, son père, Sir Thomas Harcourt, avait 43 ans et sa mère, Maud Grey, avait 40 ans. Elle a épousé Walter Cokesey environ 1400. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 1 fille. Elle est décédée en 1416, à l’âge de 31 ans.

ko.lifeSummary: Isabel de Harcourt(이)가 1385년 영국 레이스스터실르 마크트 보스워스에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Sir Thomas Harcourt(은)는 43세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Maud Grey(은)는 40세였다. 그녀는 대략 1400년 Walter Cokesey(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1416년 31세의 나이로 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Isabel de Harcourt родилась в 1385 г. в местности маркет босворт, лисестиршир, Англия, её отцу по имени Sir Thomas Harcourt было 43 года, а матери, которую звали Maud Grey, 40. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Walter Cokesey около 1400 г. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 1 дочь. Она умерла в 1416 г. в возрасте 31 год.
